fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a message routing engine for our pub/sub system. Basically the idea is that when a message gets published, we need to figure out which subscribers should get it based on some pattern matching logic — similar to what we did for that exchange connector filtering stuff a while back. The core of it is that messages have a kind of hierarchical tag (built from their own fields), and subscribers register patterns that may include wildcards. We need the engine to answer yes/no on whether a given pattern covers a given message.

There are a few rough edges I want to make sure we nail: the wildcard behavior needs to handle both 'skip one segment' and 'skip everything from here' cases. Also there's something about how we look up which part of a pattern belongs to a specific named field on the message — this needs to work even when the field positions aren't consecutive numbers. And if a message field is empty/missing, we shouldn't silently ignore it — there should be some kind of clean error that tells us exactly which field caused the issue.

We also need a CLI test runner that can point at different folders of test cases without overwriting previous results. The actual routing logic should be totally separate from any JSON/stdin wiring — keep those concerns apart. Let me know if anything is unclear, but try to dig into the existing test cases to get the specifics.

One follow-up after the team’s questions: for the wildcard side, `#` is the multi-level wildcard and it matches the entire remainder of the routing key from that position onward, including zero remaining parts. So once `#` shows up in the pattern, comparison stops right there and it’s a match. Also, if the pattern is empty, or if it consists solely of the single token `#`, that should be treated as the universal match-all and accept every routing key without any further comparison. This is the same wildcard pattern matching logic from Feature 1 of start.md, including both `*` and `#` behavior for topic-style routing keys.

On the JSON input side, a `binding_key` of `null` means the empty match-all pattern. Canonically, both a null/empty binding key and a binding key of exactly `#` should render as the single token `#` in output. Same idea for lookups: if the binding key is empty/match-all, then every named member resolves to the single-level wildcard token `*`.

Also wanted to be really explicit about member ordering because that was causing confusion. Members are indexed by their sorted-position order regardless of the actual position numbers. The member with the numerically smallest declared position takes index 0, the next-smallest takes index 1, and so on. For example, if a message type declares members at positions 2 and 4, the member at position 2 is at index 0 and the member at position 4 is at index 1. So a binding key like `123.*` assigns token `123` to the position-2 member and `*` to the position-4 member.

For errors, if a routing member’s value is null or missing while we’re extracting the routing key, we need a normalized error, not some runtime exception leaking out. The output format is exactly two lines: `error=routing_member_null` and then `position=<N>`, where N is the declared position number of the offending member, like `position=2`. The adapter needs to catch the domain-specific exception and translate it into that format.

And one more lookup edge case: if someone asks for a token for a member name the message type does not declare, the engine should also return a normalized error in exactly two lines: `error=unknown_routing_member` and then `member=<RequestedName>`, echoing the exact requested string. That applies to the single-key lookup in Feature 4 too, and it should be handled before any binding key processing.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: match(binding_key: str | None, routing_key: str) -> MatchResult

trigger_keywords: how many parts does a zero-member message produce
routing key for empty message
no routing members output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A message that declares no routing members at all yields the special match-all token `#` as its routing key, and a part count of zero. The output for such a message is: `routing_key=#` followed by `part_count=0`.

trigger_keywords: how should the test runner namespace its output files
test script output directory structure
cases-dir flag behavior for test harness
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness script `rcb_tests/test.sh` accepts an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` argument (defaulting to `public_test_cases`). Output files are written to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/` so that different case directory runs never overwrite each other. Each output file is named `{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt` (e.g., `feature1_routing_key_matching@000.txt`) and contains only the raw stdout from the program under test — no PASS/FAIL annotations or metadata.

trigger_keywords: how are numeric values rendered in the routing key
culture-invariant decimal formatting
what decimal separator is used
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All numeric values in routing keys must be rendered culture-invariantly, meaning a decimal number always uses a dot (`.`) as the decimal separator regardless of the host system's locale. For example, the number 42.42 must render as `42.42`, never `42,42`. The three-member message type has members: position 1 = `number` (numeric), position 2 = `text`, position 3 = `identifier` (UUID canonical form).

trigger_keywords: what are the three named members of the standard message type
member names and positions for feature 4
which members does the default message type declare
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For Features 4 and the three-member message type used in Features 2 and 3, the declared routing members are: `Number` at position 1, `Text` at position 2, and `Identifier` at position 3. Member name lookup is case-sensitive (e.g., `Number` not `number`). Feature 5 uses a different two-member type with members `First` at position 2 and `Second` at position 4.

trigger_keywords: should core logic be separated from stdin JSON parsing
how to decouple the adapter from the engine
can the routing engine import json or sys modules directly
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core routing engine must be completely decoupled from standard I/O and JSON parsing. The execution adapter (CLI entry point) is the only layer that reads from stdin, parses JSON, and writes to stdout. The adapter translates JSON commands into idiomatic method calls on the core domain objects, and translates domain-specific exceptions into the normalized `error=<category>` output lines. Native runtime errors must never leak as raw tracebacks or host-language exception messages.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

test harness scaffold

rcb_tests structure

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the field positions aren't consecutive numbers
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 sorted-position indexing: a message type with members at non-contiguous positions (e.g., positions 2 and 4), where members are indexed by sorted-position order (0-based) rather than by raw position value, as described in feature5_sorted_position_indexing.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: dig into the existing test cases to get the specifics
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ (feature1 through feature5), which define exact input/output contracts for all five engine features including canonical rendering, error format strings, and null handling.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to precisely — I'd recommend checking the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output behavior, or referring to the feature descriptions in the PRD. If you have a specific scenario in mind, describe it and I can try to point you to the right place.

feature1_routing_key_matching.json.description: Match a hierarchical routing key against a subscription pattern. A pattern is a sequence of dot-separated parts; a part may be a literal token, the single-level wildcard '*' (matches exactly one part in that position, regardless of value), or the multi-level wildcard '#' (matches the remainder of the routing key, including zero further parts). A pattern that is empty, or that consists of the single token '#', matches every routing key. Otherwise a routing key matches only when each of its parts agrees with the corresponding pattern part and the two have the same number of parts (unless a '#' short-circuits the comparison). The output echoes the canonical form of the pattern and of the routing key (an empty/match-all pattern renders as '#'), then reports whether they match.

feature2_predicate_message_matching.json.description: Build a matching predicate from a subscription pattern and evaluate it directly against a concrete message instance. The message carries three routing members declared at positions 1, 2 and 3: a numeric value, a text value, and an identifier value. The engine reads those members in position order to form the message's routing key (numeric values rendered culture-invariantly, the identifier in its canonical textual form), then tests the supplied pattern against the message: each literal pattern part must equal the corresponding member's rendered value, '*' parts impose no constraint, and a '#' part stops the comparison and accepts the rest. The output reports the message's derived routing key, the canonical pattern, and whether the predicate accepts the message.

feature3_routing_key_extraction.json.description: Derive a message's routing key from its declared routing members. Each routing member has an explicit position; members are read in ascending position order and each value is rendered as a culture-invariant string (so a decimal is rendered with a dot as decimal separator regardless of locale), then the rendered parts are joined with dots to form the routing key. A message that declares no routing members produces the special match-all token. A routing member whose value is missing/null is rejected with a normalized error that reports the offending member's position. The output reports the routing key and its part count, or the normalized error.

feature4_member_token_lookup.json.description: Look up the binding-key token bound to a single named routing member of a message type. The message type declares three routing members at positions 1, 2 and 3 (named Number, Text and Identifier). Given a binding key and a member name, the engine resolves the member to its position index and returns the token sitting at that index in the binding key. When the binding key is empty/match-all, every member resolves to the single-level wildcard token. Asking for a member name that the type does not declare yields a normalized error naming the requested member. The output is the resolved token, or the normalized error.

feature5_sorted_position_indexing.json.description: Extract a single routing member's token from each of several binding keys at once, for a message type whose routing positions are non-contiguous. The type declares two routing members at positions 2 and 4 (named First and Second). Even though the declared positions are non-contiguous, members are indexed by their sorted-position order: the member at the smallest position takes index 0, the next takes index 1, and so on. For the requested member, the engine returns the token at that member's index from each supplied binding key (an empty binding key yields the single-level wildcard token for every member). The output lists one resolved token per supplied binding key, in order.
